answer:
You're likely safe from CFAA criminal liability under hiQ, but LinkedIn actively sues scrapers on breach-of-contract and related state-law claims — hiQ itself ended up paying damages and destroying all its data, and LinkedIn is still suing companies like yours in 2026, so your real exposure is a costly civil suit that could force you to shut the product down.

Sources: [hiQ v. LinkedIn explained](https://www.lection.app/blogs/hiq-labs-vs-linkedin-case-explained), [Is Scraping LinkedIn Legal in 2026? (I Was Sued by LinkedIn)](https://nubela.co/blog/is-scraping-linkedin-legal-in-2026/), [Farella Braun + Martel on hiQ rulings](https://www.fbm.com/publications/what-recent-rulings-in-hiq-v-linkedin-and-other-cases-say-about-the-legality-of-data-scraping/)
